nativeRange.habitat: Shallow reef environments; rocky substrates, crevices, and reef flats. Has been documented forming extensive bubbly mats on Hawaiian reefs, most notably in Kaneohe Bay.

avoidWith: herbivorous fish that graze macroalgae heavily (e.g., tangs, rabbitfish)

imageCandidates.notes: Product photo from Mosaic Macros listing for Mosaic Snakeskin (Dictyosphaeria cavernosa).

summary: The Mosaic Snakeskin (Dictyosphaeria cavernosa) is a stiff green macroalgae from shallow Indo-Pacific reefs, identified by the small mosaic-like venations along its thallus that resemble scales or snakeskin. It forms hollow, bubble-like structures that can reach around 12 cm in diameter.
careNotes: The Mosaic Snakeskin is sometimes sold or described under the trade name 'Green Bubble Algae,' though it is distinct from the invasive bubble algae of the genus Valonia — a useful distinction to keep in mind when evaluating compatibility and pest risk. In the wild, Dictyosphaeria cavernosa has been documented forming extensive bubbly mats on Hawaiian reefs, particularly in Kaneohe Bay, typically under elevated nutrient conditions; its expansion there in the 1960s and 1970s has been linked to sewage-driven eutrophication. In home aquaria the species is generally kept as a display macroalgae at an intermediate care level. Husbandry literature for this species specifically is limited, so care recommendations are extrapolated from reef macroalgae practice generally. Standard reef water quality is advised: stable salinity around SG 1.023–1.026, temperature 23–28°C, and pH 8.1–8.4. Medium lighting is typically recommended. Growth tends to be medium-paced under adequate conditions. Propagation occurs by fragmentation — detached thallus pieces can reattach to rockwork and continue growing. As with most macroalgae collected or shipped from reef environments, specimens may arrive with hitchhiker organisms. Allelopathic effects (chemical competition with neighboring organisms) have not been documented for this genus.
breedingNotes: In aquaria, propagation occurs readily by fragmentation — detached thallus pieces can reattach and grow. Wild reproduction involves the alternation of gametophyte and sporophyte generations typical of Chlorophyta, but this cycle is not observed in captive settings.

primary.name: Mosaic Macros
primary.notes: Sole hobby trade source. Product page confirms common name, scientific name, and specimen size (1-3" cutting on plug). Description: 'stiff green macroalgae that grows with small mosaic-like venations along the thallus, resembling scales.'

additional.name: Wikipedia — Dictyosphaeria cavernosa
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy (Siphonocladaceae / Cladophorales / Ulvophyceae), morphology (hollow bubble-like structure, up to ~12 cm diameter), native range (Indo-Pacific, SE Asia, Hawaiian Kaneohe Bay), and shallow reef habitat. Single-source confirmation for biological parameters; no aquarium-specific husbandry data available.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Siphonocladaceae
additional.notes: Confirmed family placement (Siphonocladaceae, order Cladophorales) and that Dictyosphaeria is a recognized genus within the family.

additional.name: Mosaic Macros general macroalgae care guide
additional.notes: Slice-wide secondary source — provides authoritative Mosaic-wide parameter targets (temp 22-26°C, pH 8.1-8.4, Ca 400-450 ppm, Mg 1250-1350 ppm, NO3 5-20 ppm, PO4 0.02-0.1 ppm) and the 4-method propagation standard. Added retroactively 2026-06-08.
